speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Did you have other jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued to live in [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I continued to live in [INAUDIBLE]. Actually, I was living with my aunt. I helped her in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your aunt's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zita ?] Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother lived there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you told me that your mother died in Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she died of cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in the '70s. I can't remember exactly the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think you were married in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was married in Winnipeg. And then-- then after I was married and moved to Toronto-- me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry Berkelhammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason to move to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he wanted to open a business on his own. So he thought Toronto would-- would be a good city for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like coming to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very nice. I like Toronto very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know people here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know people here. But I found work, and I worked in many places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've worked at Eaton's as a sales girl. I've worked in banks, factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you began having a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your eldest child was born in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think we'll meet your children, or all but one of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: --a little later. What was your connection with the Jewish community in Toronto? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did you have a-- a connection to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE] had two synagogues. And one of the synagogues I was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your friends mostly survivors like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends among the Jews who had lived here during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it like with-- with them-- with the people who were not from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were friendly and very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've continued to live in Toronto till today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: No reason, [LAUGHS] just didn't go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think when you-- you read or hear some of the Holocaust denials-- that people who write books or articles saying there was no Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you think about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they're just phonies. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what do you think accounts for your surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are the reasons that made it possible for you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a just coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it anything to do with you? Any way that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was just coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. But you made a decision to run to the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you hadn't decided that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no way to stay at the Polish people hideouts, because they were punishing the Polish people for keeping Jewish people. And I didn't want a Polish person to suffer because of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your decision really helped all of you stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what-- how you think the Holocaust has affected you as a Jew-- your feelings about being a Jew. How has it been affected by what you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oy-- just went through a lot of horrors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does it change how you feel as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it doesn't change.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you want future generations of your family and people who might see this tape to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be proud that they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what should they be proud about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they should be proud that they're Jewish because they have a history. And Hitler was-- I mean-- a phony and a schizophrenic, and he didn't know what he was doing. And had nothing to do with Jewish heritage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what made you decide to make this tape-- why you agreed to do it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to-- to know what I went through during the war. I wanted people to know what I went through doing the war and how hard it was and how painful it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you think about that time, is there any one picture that stays in your mind? One-- any one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] scene? Any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly the picture stays the life in the woods. It was very hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do you see yourself? Where-- where are you? In the hideout?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hideout, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with my family and the young men.

speaker: interviewer
text: All crowded in together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very dark in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oy. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Dark and cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you would want to say to future grandchildren, great-grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they should be proud that they're Jewish. They should-- they should be good to their parents. That's about all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I know that you looked after your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a-- a lovely family that we'll meet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in a little while. I want to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is my father, my mother, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about three or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in Koszyce.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Koszyce. That's my mother and her second husband, Sam [? Kramer. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. That's my grandfather. He committed suicide during the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how he died. Reuben Raiselman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was dealing with wheat.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tuczyn. That's my mother, my step-father, me, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dora, Dora. She's now Jacobson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. That's my aunt, my cousin, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, Koszyce.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Koszyce. How old would you have been then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the aunt that you later lived with in Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is the aunt and the daughter that were killed by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girl's name was [? Asa, ?] and the aunt's name is [? Lata. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosen. Raiselman. That's me in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tuczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Bytom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old would you have been at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: 18. On the left is me, and on the right is my aunt, [? Janice-- ?] [? Jenny. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Jenny's ?] last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Auntie ?] [? Jenny. ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And she lives now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the aunt you lived with in Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Winnipeg and the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was actually taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bytom. That's me on the left side. And that's my uncle in Germany, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- which uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle that survived the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Raiselman. David Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he's the one who was not with you in the woods but in another hideout?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany, in-- tell me the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Lichtenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lichtenau? You were in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's me in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lichtenau. 18. That's me holding the white purse, and that's my friends. And that's Lichtenau, Germany, after the war. To the right, it's me. And those two others are my girlfriends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lichtenau, Germany, after the war. That's after the war, and that's Lichtenau, Germany. I cannot see myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Betar group that you were a member of.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you used to do at the Betar meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, making gymnastics-- all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn about Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. [NON-ENGLISH] mostly. That's me in Germany, [INAUDIBLE] Lichtenau-- after the war-- and my girlfriend, a Romanian girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bella. ?] That's me and my aunt in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the left, and she's on the right-- [? Goldie. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name [? is Goldie. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: --aunt, [? Goldie ?] Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Goldie ?] Rosen, yeah. Dave Rosen's wife. That's my uncle and aunt and his children from [INAUDIBLE], Manitoba.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your uncle Jack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his wife is?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Zita. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children-- who's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- the boy is Bob. He now owns a lumberyard in Edmonton. And the daughters, [? Shirley ?] and [? Beverly. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is [? Shirley? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Shirley ?] is the one that sits on their mother's lap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you received this photo when you were still living in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's me in Germany, [INAUDIBLE] Lichtenau-- like after-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: And friend. That's me in Germany, [INAUDIBLE] Lichtenau-- after the war. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's me in Poland after-- in Bytom. And you lived in Bytom with your mother and your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that there were many houses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have to pay rent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's me in Poland, Bytom-- me and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's-- on the right is my mother. On the left, it's me in Winnipeg-- after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know approximately what year this would have been? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember. That's me, my cousin [? Sonya, ?] and my sister in [INAUDIBLE] Lichtenau after the War in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the left is?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my sister. On the right is my cousin, [? Sonya. ?] [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Sonya ?] was the cousin whose mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wanted to choke her to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: To keep her quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep her quiet, yeah. That's from the engagement party. That's me in Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you engaged? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1954. On my right-- on the right is my father and my mother. And that's me and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're all sitting down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's engagement party. That's in Winnipeg-- me and my husband on the wedding night.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a lovely family that we'll meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you. Well, from my right is [? Paula ?] and her daughter Sasha, Rebecca, me, Ava, Sammy, Sophia, Corey, Noah, Zak, Madison, Stephanie, and Ryan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're looking at the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very happy that my bubbe survived in the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: If my bubbe didn't survive the war, then neither would any of us [INAUDIBLE] . So [INTERPOSING VOICES] I'm glad that she survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's very nice that Bubbe survived. And I would also like to say, like, it's just like all our, like, grandfathers like King David, Adam and Eve, and the Tower of Babel-- like, all those things, are all are, like, fathers. And I'm-- and if they-- and it's there, keep on going on and going and on. And if God-- and God created us. And I'm very happy that Bubbe made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Sasha, you can say something now. You can talk, if you'd like.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say you're-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] that you're happy that Bubbe's here? Do you love Bubbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love Bubbe. Glad her here. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Do you guys want a coffee or tea? [BACKROUND NOISE] [INTERPOSING VOICES] Coffee?
